label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let's try to figure this out. So the question is whether following the given instructions will bring me back to the starting point. The instructions are: always face forward. Then take 2 steps backward, then 5 steps backward, then 5 steps right, then 9 steps right. The options are Yes or No. First, I need to visualize this. Let me imagine myself standing at the starting point. I'm facing forward, which I assume is the standard direction—let's say north. So when I'm facing north, taking steps backward would be moving south. Taking steps to the right would be moving east, because if I'm facing north, right is east. So breaking down each step: 1. Take 2 steps backward: Since I'm facing north, backward is south. So I move 2 steps south from the starting point. 2. Take 5 steps backward: Again, facing north, so another 5 steps south. Total movement south so far is 2 + 5 = 7 steps. 3. Take 5 steps right: Facing north, right is east. So I move 5 steps east. 4. Take 9 steps right: Still facing north, so another 9 steps east. Total movement east is 5 + 9 = 14 steps. Now, to determine if I'm back at the starting point, I need to check if my net movement in both the north-south axis and the east-west axis is zero. In the north-south direction: I moved a total of 7 steps south. There's no movement north, so net movement is 7 steps south. In the east-west direction: I moved 14 steps east. No movement west, so net movement is 14 steps east. Since there's a net movement in both directions, I'm not back at the starting point.
